title: A Math Idea That Helped Discover Tiny Particles
summary: Long ago, a man named William Rowan Hamilton had a clever idea about math. He lived in Ireland and carved a formula on a bridge in 1843. But his important work happened earlier when he was young. He created math to study how light rays move and how objects like balls move. He thought light might be made of tiny particles, like Isaac Newton believed. Later, scientists found that light can act like both waves and particles. About 100 years after Hamilton, scientists used his math to help create quantum mechanics, which explains how tiny things in the universe work. This shows how old ideas can lead to new discoveries in science.

questions.question: What science uses Hamilton's math to explain tiny things?

background_read: William Rowan Hamilton was a mathematician and physicist from Ireland who lived in the 1800s. He made important contributions to math and science, especially in understanding motion and light. His work helped later scientists develop quantum mechanics, which studies the behavior of very small particles like atoms and photons. This story shows how ideas from long ago can still be useful today in modern science.
Article_Structure: WHO: William Rowan Hamilton, an Irish mathematician and physicist
WHAT: He created math to study light and object motion, and his ideas later helped develop quantum mechanics
WHERE: Ireland, specifically Dublin where he carved a formula on Broome Bridge
WHY: To show how old scientific ideas can lead to new discoveries about how the universe works

perspectives.description: Hamilton's work was based on older ideas from scientists like Newton, showing how science builds over time
